question_html: Using the mirror equation and the formula of magnification, deduce that "the virtual image produced by a convex mirror is always diminished in size and is located between the pole and the focus."
question_text: Using the mirror equation and the formula of magnification, deduce that "the virtual image produced by a convex mirror is always diminished in size and is located between the pole and the focus."
explanation: Let, {tex}{u}={nf}{/tex} From the Mirror formula, {tex} \frac{1}{v}+\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f} {/tex} {tex} \frac{1}{v}=\frac{1}{f}-\frac{1}{u} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{1}{f}+\frac{1}{n f} {/tex} {tex} v =\frac{n f}{n+1} {/tex} {tex} n =+v e \therefore v<f {/tex} {tex} m =\frac{-v}{u}=\frac{1}{n+1} {/tex} m is always positive & less than 1.
solution: Let, {tex}{u}={nf}{/tex} From the Mirror formula, {tex} \frac{1}{v}+\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f} {/tex} {tex} \frac{1}{v}=\frac{1}{f}-\frac{1}{u} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{1}{f}+\frac{1}{n f} {/tex} {tex} v =\frac{n f}{n+1} {/tex} {tex} n =+v e \therefore v<f {/tex} {tex} m =\frac{-v}{u}=\frac{1}{n+1} {/tex} m is always positive & less than 1.
solution_html:
Let, {tex}{u}={nf}{/tex}
From the Mirror formula,
{tex} \frac{1}{v}+\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f} {/tex}
{tex} \frac{1}{v}=\frac{1}{f}-\frac{1}{u} {/tex}
{tex} =\frac{1}{f}+\frac{1}{n f} {/tex}
{tex} v =\frac{n f}{n+1} {/tex}
{tex} n =+v e \therefore v<f {/tex}
{tex} m =\frac{-v}{u}=\frac{1}{n+1} {/tex}
m is always positive & less than 1.

question_html: A small candle, 2.5 cm in size is placed at 27 cm in front of a concave mirror of radius of curvature 36 cm. At what distance from the mirror should a screen be placed in order to obtain a sharp image? Describe the nature and size of the image. If the candle is moved closer to the mirror, how would the screen have to be moved?
question_text: A small candle, 2.5 cm in size is placed at 27 cm in front of a concave mirror of radius of curvature 36 cm. At what distance from the mirror should a screen be placed in order to obtain a sharp image? Describe the nature and size of the image. If the candle is moved closer to the mirror, how would the screen have to be moved?
explanation: The object is kept between f and C. So the image should be real, inverted and beyond C. To locate the sharp image, the screen should be placed at the position of the image. Radius of curvature of the concave mirror, R = -36 cm The focal length {tex}f=\frac{-R}{2}=-18 \mathrm{cm}{/tex} Object distance u = -27 cm Using mirror formula, {tex}\frac{1}{v}+\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{1}{v}+\frac{1}{-27}=\frac{1}{-18}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{1}{v}=-\frac{1}{18}+\frac{1}{27}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{v}=\frac{-3+2}{54} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{v}=-\frac{1}{54}{/tex} v = -54 cm Size of image can be calculated magnification {tex}m=-\frac{v}{u}=\frac{-I}{O}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{-I}{O}=-\frac{v}{u}{/tex} {tex}\frac{I}{+2.5}=-\frac{-54}{-27} \Rightarrow I=-5 \mathrm{cm}{/tex} So, the image is inverted and magnified. Thus in order to locate the sharp image, the screen should be kept 54 cm in front of the concave mirror, and the image on the screen will be observed real, inverted, and magnified. If the candle is moved closer to the mirror, the real image will move away from the mirror hence the screen has to be shifted away from the mirror to locate the sharp image.
solution: The object is kept between f and C. So the image should be real, inverted and beyond C. To locate the sharp image, the screen should be placed at the position of the image. Radius of curvature of the concave mirror, R = -36 cm The focal length {tex}f=\frac{-R}{2}=-18 \mathrm{cm}{/tex} Object distance u = -27 cm Using mirror formula, {tex}\frac{1}{v}+\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{1}{v}+\frac{1}{-27}=\frac{1}{-18}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{1}{v}=-\frac{1}{18}+\frac{1}{27}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{v}=\frac{-3+2}{54} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{v}=-\frac{1}{54}{/tex} v = -54 cm Size of image can be calculated magnification {tex}m=-\frac{v}{u}=\frac{-I}{O}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{-I}{O}=-\frac{v}{u}{/tex} {tex}\frac{I}{+2.5}=-\frac{-54}{-27} \Rightarrow I=-5 \mathrm{cm}{/tex} So, the image is inverted and magnified. Thus in order to locate the sharp image, the screen should be kept 54 cm in front of the concave mirror, and the image on the screen will be observed real, inverted, and magnified. If the candle is moved closer to the mirror, the real image will move away from the mirror hence the screen has to be shifted away from the mirror to locate the sharp image.
solution_html:
The object is kept between f and C. So the image should be real, inverted and beyond C. To locate the sharp image, the screen should be placed at the position of the image.

Radius of curvature of the concave mirror, R = -36 cm
The focal length {tex}f=\frac{-R}{2}=-18 \mathrm{cm}{/tex}
Object distance u = -27 cm
Using mirror formula, {tex}\frac{1}{v}+\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f}{/tex}
or {tex}\frac{1}{v}+\frac{1}{-27}=\frac{1}{-18}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{1}{v}=-\frac{1}{18}+\frac{1}{27}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{1}{v}=\frac{-3+2}{54} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{v}=-\frac{1}{54}{/tex}
v = -54 cm
Size of image can be calculated magnification
{tex}m=-\frac{v}{u}=\frac{-I}{O}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{-I}{O}=-\frac{v}{u}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{I}{+2.5}=-\frac{-54}{-27} \Rightarrow I=-5 \mathrm{cm}{/tex}
So, the image is inverted and magnified. Thus in order to locate the sharp image, the screen should be kept 54 cm in front of the concave mirror, and the image on the screen will be observed real, inverted, and magnified. If the candle is moved closer to the mirror, the real image will move away from the mirror hence the screen has to be shifted away from the mirror to locate the sharp image.

question_html: Consider a convex spherical surface of radius of curvature R, separating two media of refractive indices n1 and n2 (> n1). A point object is placed at a distance u in front of the surface in medium of refractive index n1. Its real image is formed at a distance v. Obtain a relation between u and v in terms of n1, n2 and R.
question_text: Consider a convex spherical surface of radius of curvature R, separating two media of refractive indices n 1 and n 2 (> n 1 ). A point object is placed at a distance u in front of the surface in medium of refractive index n 1 . Its real image is formed at a distance v. Obtain a relation between u and v in terms of n 1 , n 2 and R.
explanation: for small angles, tan {tex} \angle \mathrm{NOM}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{M N}{O M}{/tex} tan {tex}\angle \mathrm{NCM}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{M N}{M C}{/tex} tan {tex}\angle \mathrm{NIM}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{M N}{M I}{/tex} Now, for {tex}\triangle{/tex} NOC, {tex}\angle {\text i}{/tex} i is the exterior angle. Therefore, {tex}\angle {\text i}{/tex} = {tex}\angle \mathrm{NOM}{/tex} + {tex}\angle \mathrm{NCM}{/tex} i = {tex}\frac{M N}{O M}{/tex} + {tex}\frac{M N}{M C}{/tex} Similarly, r = {tex}\angle \mathrm{NCM}{/tex} - {tex}\angle \mathrm{NIM}{/tex} i.e. r = {tex}\frac{M N}{M C}{/tex} - {tex}\frac{M N}{M I}{/tex} By Snell's law n 1 sin i = n 2 sin r or for small angles, sin i {tex}\approx{/tex} i and sin r {tex}\approx{/tex} r n 1 i = n 2 r Substituting i and r, we get {tex}\frac{n_1}{O M}{/tex} + {tex}\frac{n_2}{M I}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{n_2-n_1}{M C}{/tex} Where OM, MI and MC represent magnitude of distances. Applying the Cartesian sign convention OM = -u, MI = +v, MC = +R Substituting these in equation, we get {tex}\frac{n_2}{v}{/tex} - {tex}\frac{n_1}{u}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{n_2-n_1}{R}{/tex}
solution: for small angles, tan {tex} \angle \mathrm{NOM}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{M N}{O M}{/tex} tan {tex}\angle \mathrm{NCM}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{M N}{M C}{/tex} tan {tex}\angle \mathrm{NIM}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{M N}{M I}{/tex} Now, for {tex}\triangle{/tex} NOC, {tex}\angle {\text i}{/tex} i is the exterior angle. Therefore, {tex}\angle {\text i}{/tex} = {tex}\angle \mathrm{NOM}{/tex} + {tex}\angle \mathrm{NCM}{/tex} i = {tex}\frac{M N}{O M}{/tex} + {tex}\frac{M N}{M C}{/tex} Similarly, r = {tex}\angle \mathrm{NCM}{/tex} - {tex}\angle \mathrm{NIM}{/tex} i.e. r = {tex}\frac{M N}{M C}{/tex} - {tex}\frac{M N}{M I}{/tex} By Snell's law n 1 sin i = n 2 sin r or for small angles, sin i {tex}\approx{/tex} i and sin r {tex}\approx{/tex} r n 1 i = n 2 r Substituting i and r, we get {tex}\frac{n_1}{O M}{/tex} + {tex}\frac{n_2}{M I}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{n_2-n_1}{M C}{/tex} Where OM, MI and MC represent magnitude of distances. Applying the Cartesian sign convention OM = -u, MI = +v, MC = +R Substituting these in equation, we get {tex}\frac{n_2}{v}{/tex} - {tex}\frac{n_1}{u}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{n_2-n_1}{R}{/tex}
solution_html:
for small angles,
tan {tex} \angle \mathrm{NOM}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{M N}{O M}{/tex}
tan {tex}\angle \mathrm{NCM}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{M N}{M C}{/tex}
tan {tex}\angle \mathrm{NIM}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{M N}{M I}{/tex}
Now, for {tex}\triangle{/tex}NOC, {tex}\angle {\text i}{/tex} i is the exterior angle. Therefore, {tex}\angle {\text i}{/tex} = {tex}\angle \mathrm{NOM}{/tex} + {tex}\angle \mathrm{NCM}{/tex}
i = {tex}\frac{M N}{O M}{/tex} + {tex}\frac{M N}{M C}{/tex}
Similarly,
r = {tex}\angle \mathrm{NCM}{/tex} - {tex}\angle \mathrm{NIM}{/tex}
i.e. r = {tex}\frac{M N}{M C}{/tex} - {tex}\frac{M N}{M I}{/tex}
By Snell's law
n1 sin i = n2 sin r
or for small angles, sin i {tex}\approx{/tex} i and sin r {tex}\approx{/tex} r
n1 i = n2 r
Substituting i and r, we get
{tex}\frac{n_1}{O M}{/tex} + {tex}\frac{n_2}{M I}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{n_2-n_1}{M C}{/tex}
Where OM, MI and MC represent magnitude of distances.
Applying the Cartesian sign convention
OM = -u, MI = +v, MC = +R
Substituting these in equation, we get
{tex}\frac{n_2}{v}{/tex} - {tex}\frac{n_1}{u}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{n_2-n_1}{R}{/tex}

question_text: Using the necessary ray diagram, derive the mirror formula for a concave mirror. In the magnified image of a measuring scale (with equidistant markings) lying along the principal axis of a concave mirror, the markings are not equidistant. Explain.
explanation: Mirror formula: M 1 M 2 is a concave mirror having pole P, focus F and centre of curvature C. An object AB is placed in front of mirror with point B on the principal axis. The image formed by mirror is A'B'. The perpendicular dropped from point of incidence D on the principal axis is DN. In {tex}\triangle{/tex} ABC and {tex}\triangle{/tex} A'B'C {tex}\angle{/tex} ABC = {tex}\angle{/tex} A'B'C (each equal to 90 o ) {tex}\angle{/tex} ACB = {tex}\angle{/tex} A'CB'(opposite angles) Both triangles are similar. {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{A B}{A^{\prime} B^{\prime}}=\frac{B C}{B^{\prime} C}{/tex} ...(i) Now in {tex}\triangle{/tex} DNF and {tex}\triangle{/tex} A'B'F {tex}\angle{/tex} DNF = {tex}\angle{/tex} A'B'F (each equal to 90 o ) {tex}\angle{/tex} DFN = {tex}\angle{/tex} A'FB' (oppsite angles) {tex}\therefore{/tex} Both triangles are similar {tex}\frac{D N}{A^{\prime} B^{\prime}}=\frac{F N}{B^{\prime} F}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{A B}{A^{\prime} B^{\prime}}=\frac{F N}{B^{\prime} F}{/tex} ( {tex}\because{/tex} AB = DN) ...(ii) Comparing (i) and (ii), we get {tex}\frac{B C}{B^{\prime} C}=\frac{F N}{B^{\prime} F}{/tex} ...(iii) If the aperture of the mirror is very small, the point N will be very near to P, so FN = FP {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{B C}{B^{\prime} C}=\frac{F P}{B^{\prime} F}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{P B-P C}{P C-P B^{\prime}}=\frac{F P}{P B^{\prime}-P F}{/tex} ...(iv) sign convention Distance of object from mirror PB = -u Distance of image from mirror PB' = -v Focal length of mirror PF = -F Radius of curvature of mirror PC = -R = -2 f Substituting these values in (4), we get {tex}\frac{-u-(-2 f)}{-2 f-(-v)}=\frac{-f}{-v-(-f)}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{-u+2 f}{-2 f+v}=\frac{-f}{-v+f}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2f 2 - vf = -uf + uv + 2f 2 - 2fv or fv + uf = uv Dividing both sides by uvf, we get {tex}\frac{1}{f}=\frac{1}{v}+\frac{1}{u}{/tex} In the magnified image of a measuring scale lying along the principal axis of a concave mirror, the markings are not equidistant, Because it reflects each marking differently you could say a concave mirror converges the ray of light approaching from the object and in that way, it reflects each marking on the measuring scale differently.

question_text: In the given figure, for what value of {tex}\angle{/tex} i should a ray of light be incident on the face of a prism of refracting angle 60°, so that it just suffers total internal reflection at other face? (For prism, {tex}\mu{/tex} = 1.524)
explanation: According to the question, light ray suffers total internal reflection at the second surface. So, if we observe the figure, then we find that angle of emergence, e = 90°. Now, applying Snell’s law at face AC, we have {tex}\frac { \sin e } { \sin r _ { 2 } } = \mu{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \sin r _ { 2 } = \frac { 1 } { \mu } \times \sin 90 ^ { \circ }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \sin r _ { 2 } = \frac { 1 } { 1.524 } \times 1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}r_{2} = sin^{-1}(0.656){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}r_{2} = 41°{/tex} From geometry, {tex}\angle A = \angle r _ { 1 } + \angle r _ { 2 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \angle r _ { 1 } = \angle A - \angle r _ { 2 }{/tex} = 60° - 41° = 19° Applying Snell’s law at face AB, we have {tex}\mu = \frac { \sin i } { \sin r _ { 1 } }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \sin i = ( \mu ) \sin r _ { 1 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} sin i = 1.524 {tex}\times{/tex} sin 19° {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} i = sin -1 (0.496) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} i = 29.74° So angle of incidence must be equal to 29.74 degrees for total internal reflection to occur at the interface.

solution_html:
According to the question, light ray suffers total internal reflection at the second surface. So, if we observe the figure, then we find that angle of emergence, e = 90°.
Now, applying Snell’s law at face AC, we have
{tex}\frac { \sin e } { \sin r _ { 2 } } = \mu{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \sin r _ { 2 } = \frac { 1 } { \mu } \times \sin 90 ^ { \circ }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \sin r _ { 2 } = \frac { 1 } { 1.524 } \times 1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}{tex}r_{2} = sin^{-1}(0.656){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}{tex}r_{2} = 41°{/tex}
From geometry,
{tex}\angle A = \angle r _ { 1 } + \angle r _ { 2 }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \angle r _ { 1 } = \angle A - \angle r _ { 2 }{/tex}
= 60° - 41°
= 19°
Applying Snell’s law at face AB, we have
{tex}\mu = \frac { \sin i } { \sin r _ { 1 } }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \sin i = ( \mu ) \sin r _ { 1 }{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} i = 29.74°
So angle of incidence must be equal to 29.74 degrees for total internal reflection to occur at the interface.

    Hence the ray would not suffer total internal reflection on striking the face AC

question_html: At what angle should a ray of light be incident on the face of a prism of refracting angle 60° so that it just suffers total internal reflection at the other face? The refractive index of the material of the prism is 1.524
question_text: At what angle should a ray of light be incident on the face of a prism of refracting angle 60° so that it just suffers total internal reflection at the other face? The refractive index of the material of the prism is 1.524
explanation: The beam should be incident at critical angle or more than critical angle, for total internal reflection at second surface of the prism. Let us first find critical angle for air glass interface. We know {tex}\sin C=\frac{1}{^{a} \mu_{g}}{/tex} {tex}C=\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{a \mu_{g}}\right)=\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{1.524}\right){/tex} Critical angle C = 41 o Now we can calculate 'r', as the refracted ray in the prism is incident on the second face at critical angle i c. thus, 60 o + (90 o - r) + (90 o - C) = 180 o or , r = 19 o Using Snell's law, required angle of incidence i at first surface can be calculated. {tex}^{a} \mu_{g}=\frac{\sin i}{\sin r}{/tex} or {tex}1.524=\frac{\sin i}{\sin 19^{\circ}}{/tex} sin i = 1.524 (sin 19 o ) sin i = 1.524 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.3256 {tex}\Rightarrow i \cong 29.75^{\circ}{/tex}
solution: The beam should be incident at critical angle or more than critical angle, for total internal reflection at second surface of the prism. Let us first find critical angle for air glass interface. We know {tex}\sin C=\frac{1}{^{a} \mu_{g}}{/tex} {tex}C=\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{a \mu_{g}}\right)=\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{1.524}\right){/tex} Critical angle C = 41 o Now we can calculate 'r', as the refracted ray in the prism is incident on the second face at critical angle i c. thus, 60 o + (90 o - r) + (90 o - C) = 180 o or , r = 19 o Using Snell's law, required angle of incidence i at first surface can be calculated. {tex}^{a} \mu_{g}=\frac{\sin i}{\sin r}{/tex} or {tex}1.524=\frac{\sin i}{\sin 19^{\circ}}{/tex} sin i = 1.524 (sin 19 o ) sin i = 1.524 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.3256 {tex}\Rightarrow i \cong 29.75^{\circ}{/tex}
solution_html:
The beam should be incident at critical angle or more than critical angle, for total internal reflection at second surface of the prism.

Let us first find critical angle for air glass interface.
We know
{tex}\sin C=\frac{1}{^{a} \mu_{g}}{/tex}
{tex}C=\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{a \mu_{g}}\right)=\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{1.524}\right){/tex}
Critical angle C = 41o
Now we can calculate 'r', as the refracted ray in the prism is incident on the second face at critical angle ic. thus,
60o + (90o - r) + (90o - C) = 180o
or , r = 19o
Using Snell's law, required angle of incidence i at first surface can be calculated.
{tex}^{a} \mu_{g}=\frac{\sin i}{\sin r}{/tex} or {tex}1.524=\frac{\sin i}{\sin 19^{\circ}}{/tex}
sin i = 1.524 (sin 19o)

 sin i = 1.524 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.3256 {tex}\Rightarrow i \cong 29.75^{\circ}{/tex}

question_html:
A card sheet divided into squares each of size 1 mm2 is being viewed at a distance of 9 cm through a magnifying glass (a converging lens of focal length 9 cm) held close to the eye.

  1. What is the magnification produced by the lens? How much is the area of each square in the virtual image?
  2. What is the angular magnification (magnifying power) of the lens?
  3. Is the magnification in (a) equal to the magnifying power in (b)? Explain.
question_text: A card sheet divided into squares each of size 1 mm 2 is being viewed at a distance of 9 cm through a magnifying glass (a converging lens of focal length 9 cm) held close to the eye. What is the magnification produced by the lens? How much is the area of each square in the virtual image? What is the angular magnification (magnifying power) of the lens? Is the magnification in (a) equal to the magnifying power in (b)? Explain.
explanation: Area of each square, A = 1 mm 2 Object distance, u = -9 cm Focal length of a converging lens, f = 9 cm For image distance v, the lens formula can be written as; {tex}\frac{1}{f}=\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{u}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{9}=\frac{1}{v}+\frac{1}{9}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{v}=-\frac{1}{81}{/tex} {tex}\therefore v=-81 \mathrm{cm}{/tex} Magnification is given by, {tex}m=\frac{v}{u}=\frac{-81}{-9}=9{/tex} Area of each square in the virtual image = (10) 2 A = 10 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 = 100 mm 2 = 1 cm 2 Magnifying power of the lens = {tex}\frac{d}{|u|}=\frac{25}{9}{/tex} = 2.8 No, magnification of an image by a lens and angular magnification (or magnifying power) of an optical instrument are two separate things. The magnification in (a) is not the same as the magnifying power in (b) The magnification magnitude is {tex}\left(\left|\frac{v}{u}\right|\right){/tex} and the magnifying power is {tex}\left(\frac{d}{|u|}\right){/tex} . The two quantities will be equal when the images is formed at the near point (25 cm).
solution: Area of each square, A = 1 mm 2 Object distance, u = -9 cm Focal length of a converging lens, f = 9 cm For image distance v, the lens formula can be written as; {tex}\frac{1}{f}=\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{u}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{9}=\frac{1}{v}+\frac{1}{9}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{v}=-\frac{1}{81}{/tex} {tex}\therefore v=-81 \mathrm{cm}{/tex} Magnification is given by, {tex}m=\frac{v}{u}=\frac{-81}{-9}=9{/tex} Area of each square in the virtual image = (10) 2 A = 10 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 = 100 mm 2 = 1 cm 2 Magnifying power of the lens = {tex}\frac{d}{|u|}=\frac{25}{9}{/tex} = 2.8 No, magnification of an image by a lens and angular magnification (or magnifying power) of an optical instrument are two separate things. The magnification in (a) is not the same as the magnifying power in (b) The magnification magnitude is {tex}\left(\left|\frac{v}{u}\right|\right){/tex} and the magnifying power is {tex}\left(\frac{d}{|u|}\right){/tex} . The two quantities will be equal when the images is formed at the near point (25 cm).
solution_html:
1. Area of each square, A = 1 mm2
    Object distance, u = -9 cm
    Focal length of a converging lens, f = 9 cm
    For image distance v, the lens formula can be written as;
    {tex}\frac{1}{f}=\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{u}{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{1}{9}=\frac{1}{v}+\frac{1}{9}{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{1}{v}=-\frac{1}{81}{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore v=-81 \mathrm{cm}{/tex}
    Magnification is given by, {tex}m=\frac{v}{u}=\frac{-81}{-9}=9{/tex}
    Area of each square in the virtual image = (10)2A = 102 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 = 100 mm2 = 1 cm2
  2. Magnifying power of the lens = {tex}\frac{d}{|u|}=\frac{25}{9}{/tex} = 2.8
  3. No, magnification of an image by a lens and angular magnification (or magnifying power) of an optical instrument are two separate things. The magnification in (a) is not the same as the magnifying power in (b) The magnification magnitude is {tex}\left(\left|\frac{v}{u}\right|\right){/tex} and the magnifying power is {tex}\left(\frac{d}{|u|}\right){/tex}. The two quantities will be equal when the images is formed at the near point (25 cm).

question_text: Explain with the help of a labelled ray diagram the formation of final image by an astronomical telescope at infinity. Write the expression for its magnifying power. The total magnification produced by a compound microscope is 20. The magnification produced by the eyepiece is 5. When the microscope is focussed on a certain object, the distance between the objective and eyepiece is observed to be 14 cm. Calculate the focal lengths of the objective and the eyepiece. (Given that the least distance of distinct vision {tex}=25 {~cm}{/tex} )
explanation: Light from distant object enters the objective lens \& forms a real image A'B' at {tex}f_o{/tex} . This image {tex}A^{\prime} B^{\prime}{/tex} acts as an object for eye piece and eye piece forms a magnified image at infinity. {tex} \text { Magnifying Power }=f_o / f_e {/tex} Image is formed at least distance of distinct vision {tex} 20=m_o \times m_e {/tex} {tex} m_o=\frac{20}{5}=4 {/tex} {tex} m_e=1+\frac{D}{f_e} {/tex} {tex} f_e=\frac{25}{4} {~cm} {/tex} {tex} \frac{1}{{v}_{{e}}}-\frac{1}{u_e}=\frac{1}{f_e} {/tex} {tex} \frac{1}{-25}-\frac{1}{u_e}=\frac{4}{25} {/tex} {tex} u_e=-5 {~cm} {/tex} {tex} L={v}_0+\left|u_e\right| {/tex} {tex} {v}_0=9 {~cm} {/tex} Given, {tex}\frac{{v}_0}{u_0}=4{/tex} {tex} \frac{1}{{v}_0}-\frac{1}{u_0}=\frac{1}{f_0} {/tex} {tex} \frac{1}{f_0}=\frac{1}{9}-\left(-\frac{4}{9}\right) {/tex} {tex} f_0=\frac{9}{5} {~cm} {/tex}
solution: Light from distant object enters the objective lens \& forms a real image A'B' at {tex}f_o{/tex} . This image {tex}A^{\prime} B^{\prime}{/tex} acts as an object for eye piece and eye piece forms a magnified image at infinity. {tex} \text { Magnifying Power }=f_o / f_e {/tex} Image is formed at least distance of distinct vision {tex} 20=m_o \times m_e {/tex} {tex} m_o=\frac{20}{5}=4 {/tex} {tex} m_e=1+\frac{D}{f_e} {/tex} {tex} f_e=\frac{25}{4} {~cm} {/tex} {tex} \frac{1}{{v}_{{e}}}-\frac{1}{u_e}=\frac{1}{f_e} {/tex} {tex} \frac{1}{-25}-\frac{1}{u_e}=\frac{4}{25} {/tex} {tex} u_e=-5 {~cm} {/tex} {tex} L={v}_0+\left|u_e\right| {/tex} {tex} {v}_0=9 {~cm} {/tex} Given, {tex}\frac{{v}_0}{u_0}=4{/tex} {tex} \frac{1}{{v}_0}-\frac{1}{u_0}=\frac{1}{f_0} {/tex} {tex} \frac{1}{f_0}=\frac{1}{9}-\left(-\frac{4}{9}\right) {/tex} {tex} f_0=\frac{9}{5} {~cm} {/tex}
solution_html:
1. Light from distant object enters the objective lens \& forms a real image A'B' at {tex}f_o{/tex}.
    This image {tex}A^{\prime} B^{\prime}{/tex} acts as an object for eye piece and eye piece forms a magnified image at infinity.
    {tex} \text { Magnifying Power }=f_o / f_e {/tex}
  2. Image is formed at least distance of distinct vision
    {tex} 20=m_o \times m_e {/tex}
    {tex} m_o=\frac{20}{5}=4 {/tex}
    {tex} m_e=1+\frac{D}{f_e} {/tex}
    {tex} f_e=\frac{25}{4} {~cm} {/tex}
    {tex} \frac{1}{{v}_{{e}}}-\frac{1}{u_e}=\frac{1}{f_e} {/tex}
    {tex} \frac{1}{-25}-\frac{1}{u_e}=\frac{4}{25} {/tex}
    {tex} u_e=-5 {~cm} {/tex}
    {tex} L={v}_0+\left|u_e\right| {/tex}
    {tex} {v}_0=9 {~cm} {/tex}
    Given, {tex}\frac{{v}_0}{u_0}=4{/tex}
    {tex} \frac{1}{{v}_0}-\frac{1}{u_0}=\frac{1}{f_0} {/tex}
    {tex} \frac{1}{f_0}=\frac{1}{9}-\left(-\frac{4}{9}\right) {/tex}
    {tex} f_0=\frac{9}{5} {~cm} {/tex}

question_html:
1. Obtain lens maker's formula using the expression
    {tex}\frac { \mu _ { 2 } } { v } - \frac { \mu _ { 1 } } { u } = \frac { \left( \mu _ { 2 } - \mu _ { 1 } \right) } { R }{/tex}
    Here, the ray of light propagating from a rarer medium of refractive index ({tex}\mu_1{/tex}) to a denser medium of refractive index ({tex}\mu_2{/tex}) is incident on the convex side of spherical refracting surface of radius of curvature R.
  2. Draw a ray diagram to show that image formation by a concave mirror when the object is kept between its focus and the pole. Using this diagram, derive the magnification formula for the image formed.
question_text: Obtain lens maker's formula using the expression {tex}\frac { \mu _ { 2 } } { v } - \frac { \mu _ { 1 } } { u } = \frac { \left( \mu _ { 2 } - \mu _ { 1 } \right) } { R }{/tex} Here, the ray of light propagating from a rarer medium of refractive index ( {tex}\mu_1{/tex} ) to a denser medium of refractive index ( {tex}\mu_2{/tex} ) is incident on the convex side of spherical refracting surface of radius of curvature R. Draw a ray diagram to show that image formation by a concave mirror when the object is kept between its focus and the pole. Using this diagram, derive the magnification formula for the image formed.
explanation: Consider the ray diagram shown below: For refraction at the first surface {tex}\frac{\mu_2}{v_1}{/tex} - {tex}\frac{\mu_1}{u}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_2 - \mu_1}{R_1}{/tex} ....(i) For the second surface, I 1 acts as a virtual object (located in the denser medium) whose final real image is formed in the rarer medium at I. So for refraction at this surface, we have {tex}\frac{\mu_1}{v}{/tex} - {tex}\frac{\mu_2}{v_1}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_1 - \mu_2}{R_2}{/tex} ....(ii) From equations (i) and (ii), {tex}\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{u}{/tex} = {tex}\left(\frac{\mu_{2}}{\mu_{1}}-1\right)\left(\frac{1}{\mathrm{R}_{1}}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{R}_{2}}\right){/tex} The point, where image of an object, located at infinity is formed, is called the focus F, of the lens and the distance f gives its focal length.   2. In a compound microscope, an object is placed at a distance of 1.5 cm from the objective of focal length 1.25 cm. The eyepiece has a focal length of 5 cm. The final image is formed at infinity. Calculate the distance between the objective and the eyepiece.
question_text: Draw a ray diagram to show the image formation by a compound microscope. Obtain the expression for the total magnification of the microscope when the final image is formed at infinity. In a compound microscope, an object is placed at a distance of 1.5 cm from the objective of focal length 1.25 cm. The eyepiece has a focal length of 5 cm. The final image is formed at infinity. Calculate the distance between the objective and the eyepiece.
explanation: Magnification produced by objective {tex}{m}_{0}=\frac{{h}^{\prime}}{{h}}=\frac{{L}}{{f}_{{o}}}{/tex} Magnification produced by eye-piece {tex}{m}_{{e}}=1+\frac{{D}}{{f}_{{e}}}{/tex} If the final image is formed at infinity {tex}{m}_{{e}}=\frac{{D}}{{f}_{{e}}}{/tex} Total magnification {tex}{m}={m}_{0} \times {m}_{{e}}{/tex} {tex}=\left(\frac{L}{f_{o}}\right)\left(\frac{D}{f_{e}}\right){/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{{v}_{{o}}}-\frac{1}{{u}_{{o}}}=\frac{1}{{f}_{0}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{{v}_{{o}}}-\frac{1}{(-1.5)}=\frac{1}{1.25}{/tex} {tex}{v}_{{o}}=7.5 {~cm}{/tex} {tex}{L}=\left|{v}_{{o}}\right|+\left|{f}_{{e}}\right| {/tex} {tex}\text { as final image is formed at infinity }{/tex} {tex}\left({v}_{e}=\infty, u_{e}=f_{e}\right){/tex} {tex}{L}=7.5+5{/tex} {tex}{~L}=12.5 {~cm}{/tex}
solution: Magnification produced by objective {tex}{m}_{0}=\frac{{h}^{\prime}}{{h}}=\frac{{L}}{{f}_{{o}}}{/tex} Magnification produced by eye-piece {tex}{m}_{{e}}=1+\frac{{D}}{{f}_{{e}}}{/tex} If the final image is formed at infinity {tex}{m}_{{e}}=\frac{{D}}{{f}_{{e}}}{/tex} Total magnification {tex}{m}={m}_{0} \times {m}_{{e}}{/tex} {tex}=\left(\frac{L}{f_{o}}\right)\left(\frac{D}{f_{e}}\right){/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{{v}_{{o}}}-\frac{1}{{u}_{{o}}}=\frac{1}{{f}_{0}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{{v}_{{o}}}-\frac{1}{(-1.5)}=\frac{1}{1.25}{/tex} {tex}{v}_{{o}}=7.5 {~cm}{/tex} {tex}{L}=\left|{v}_{{o}}\right|+\left|{f}_{{e}}\right| {/tex} {tex}\text { as final image is formed at infinity }{/tex} {tex}\left({v}_{e}=\infty, u_{e}=f_{e}\right){/tex} {tex}{L}=7.5+5{/tex} {tex}{~L}=12.5 {~cm}{/tex}

    {tex}{m}_{0}=\frac{{h}^{\prime}}{{h}}=\frac{{L}}{{f}_{{o}}}{/tex}

1. Explain the working principle of an optical fibre with the help of a diagram. Mention one use of a light pipe.
  2. A ray of light is incident at an angle of 60o on one face of a prism with the prism angle A = 60o. The ray passes symmetrically through the prism. Find the angle of minimum deviation {tex}\left(\delta_m\right){/tex} and refractive index of the material of the prism. If the prism is immersed in water, how will {tex}\delta_m{/tex} be affected? Justify your answer.
question_text: Explain the working principle of an optical fibre with the help of a diagram. Mention one use of a light pipe. A ray of light is incident at an angle of 60 o on one face of a prism with the prism angle A = 60 o . The ray passes symmetrically through the prism. Find the angle of minimum deviation {tex}\left(\delta_m\right){/tex} and refractive index of the material of the prism. If the prism is immersed in water, how will {tex}\delta_m{/tex} be affected? Justify your answer.
explanation: Working Principle - Optical fibre uses the principle of total internal reflection to capture the light transmitted in an optical fibre and confine the light to the core of the fibre. Uses: i)Transmission of audio and video signal ii) Examination of internal organs iii) Endoscopy the angle of minimum deviation {tex}\delta_m{/tex} = i + e - A {tex}\delta_m{/tex} = 2i - A {tex}\delta_m{/tex} = 60 o Refractive Index is given by {tex}\mu=\frac{\sin \left(\frac{A+\delta_m}{2}\right)}{\sin \frac{A}{2}}{/tex} {tex}\mu=\frac{\sin \frac{120^{\circ}}{2}}{\sin \frac{60^{\circ}}{2}}{/tex} {tex}\mu=\frac{\sin 60^{\circ}}{\sin 30^{\circ}}=\frac{\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}}{\frac{1}{2}}{/tex} {tex}\mu=\sqrt{3}{/tex} If the prism is immersed in water {tex}\mu{/tex} decreases and consequently angle of minimum deviation decreases. Since {tex}\delta_m{/tex} depends on {tex}\mu{/tex} through equation given above.
solution: Working Principle - Optical fibre uses the principle of total internal reflection to capture the light transmitted in an optical fibre and confine the light to the core of the fibre. Uses: i)Transmission of audio and video signal ii) Examination of internal organs iii) Endoscopy the angle of minimum deviation {tex}\delta_m{/tex} = i + e - A {tex}\delta_m{/tex} = 2i - A {tex}\delta_m{/tex} = 60 o Refractive Index is given by {tex}\mu=\frac{\sin \left(\frac{A+\delta_m}{2}\right)}{\sin \frac{A}{2}}{/tex} {tex}\mu=\frac{\sin \frac{120^{\circ}}{2}}{\sin \frac{60^{\circ}}{2}}{/tex} {tex}\mu=\frac{\sin 60^{\circ}}{\sin 30^{\circ}}=\frac{\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}}{\frac{1}{2}}{/tex} {tex}\mu=\sqrt{3}{/tex} If the prism is immersed in water {tex}\mu{/tex} decreases and consequently angle of minimum deviation decreases. Since {tex}\delta_m{/tex} depends on {tex}\mu{/tex} through equation given above.
solution_html:
1. Working Principle - Optical fibre uses the principle of total internal reflection to capture the light transmitted in an optical fibre and confine the light to the core of the fibre.

    Uses: i)Transmission of audio and video signal
    ii) Examination of internal organs
    iii) Endoscopy
  2. the angle of minimum deviation 
    {tex}\delta_m{/tex} = i + e - A
    {tex}\delta_m{/tex} = 2i - A
    {tex}\delta_m{/tex} = 60o
    Refractive Index is given by
    {tex}\mu=\frac{\sin \left(\frac{A+\delta_m}{2}\right)}{\sin \frac{A}{2}}{/tex}
    {tex}\mu=\frac{\sin \frac{120^{\circ}}{2}}{\sin \frac{60^{\circ}}{2}}{/tex}
    {tex}\mu=\frac{\sin 60^{\circ}}{\sin 30^{\circ}}=\frac{\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}}{\frac{1}{2}}{/tex}
    {tex}\mu=\sqrt{3}{/tex}
    If the prism is immersed in water {tex}\mu{/tex} decreases and consequently angle of minimum deviation decreases. Since {tex}\delta_m{/tex} depends on {tex}\mu{/tex} through equation given above.

question_html: Draw a ray diagram to show the formation of the image of an object placed on the axis of a convex refracting surface of radius of curvature 'R', separating the two media of refractive indices 'µ1' and 'µ2' (µ2 > µ1). Use this diagram to deduce the relation {tex}\frac{\mu_2}{v} - \frac{\mu_1}{u} = \frac{\mu_2 -\mu_1}{R} {/tex}, where u and v represent respectively the distance of the object and the image formed.
question_text: Draw a ray diagram to show the formation of the image of an object placed on the axis of a convex refracting surface of radius of curvature 'R', separating the two media of refractive indices 'µ 1 ' and 'µ 2 ' (µ 2 > µ 1 ). Use this diagram to deduce the relation {tex}\frac{\mu_2}{v} - \frac{\mu_1}{u} = \frac{\mu_2 -\mu_1}{R} {/tex} , where u and v represent respectively the distance of the object and the image formed.
explanation: In case of refraction at convex spherical surface, when object is in rarer medium, the image is formed real. In {tex}\triangle{/tex} OAC, i = a + {tex}\gamma {/tex} and in {tex}\Delta A I C , \gamma = r + \beta \text { or } r = \gamma - \beta{/tex} Therefore, By snell's law, we have {tex}^ { 1 } \mu _ { 2 } = \frac { \sin i } { \sin r } \approx \frac { i } { r } = \frac { \alpha + \gamma } { \gamma - \beta }{/tex} or {tex}\frac { \mu _ { 2 } } { \mu _ { 1 } } = \frac { \alpha + \gamma } { \gamma - \beta } \text { or } \mu _ { 2 } \gamma - \mu _ { 2 } \beta = \mu _ { 1 } \alpha + \mu _ { 1 } \gamma{/tex} or {tex}\left( \mu _ { 2 } - \mu _ { 1 } \right) \gamma = \mu _ { 1 } \alpha + \mu _ { 2 } \beta{/tex} ................(i) As {tex}\alpha , \beta{/tex} and {tex}\gamma {/tex} are small and P and N lie close to each other. So, {tex}\alpha \approx \tan \alpha = \frac { A N } { N O } \approx \frac { A N } { P O }{/tex} {tex}\beta \approx \tan \beta = \frac { A N } { N I } \approx \frac { A N } { P I }{/tex} {tex}\gamma \approx \tan \gamma = \frac { A N } { N C } \approx \frac { A N } { P C }{/tex} On using them in equation (i), we obtain {tex}\left( \mu _ { 2 } - \mu _ { 1 } \right) \frac { A N } { P C } = \mu _ { 1 } \frac { A N } { P O } + \mu _ { 2 } \frac { A N } { P I }{/tex} or {tex}\frac { \mu _ { 2 } - \mu _ { 1 } } { P C } = \frac { \mu _ { 1 } } { P O } + \frac { \mu _ { 2 } } { P I }{/tex} ..........(ii) where, PC = +R, radius of curvature PO = -u, object distance PI = +v, image distance So, {tex}\frac { \mu _ { 2 } - \mu _ { 1 } } { R } = \frac { \mu _ { 1 } } { - u } + \frac { \mu _ { 2 } } { v } \text { or } \frac { \mu _ { 2 } - \mu _ { 1 } } { R } = \frac { \mu _ { 2 } } { v } - \frac { \mu _ { 1 } } { u }{/tex} This gives formula for refraction at spherical surface, when object is in rarer medium.
solution: In case of refraction at convex spherical surface, when object is in rarer medium, the image is formed real. In {tex}\triangle{/tex} OAC, i = a + {tex}\gamma {/tex} and in {tex}\Delta A I C , \gamma = r + \beta \text { or } r = \gamma - \beta{/tex} Therefore, By snell's law, we have {tex}^ { 1 } \mu _ { 2 } = \frac { \sin i } { \sin r } \approx \frac { i } { r } = \frac { \alpha + \gamma } { \gamma - \beta }{/tex} or {tex}\frac { \mu _ { 2 } } { \mu _ { 1 } } = \frac { \alpha + \gamma } { \gamma - \beta } \text { or } \mu _ { 2 } \gamma - \mu _ { 2 } \beta = \mu _ { 1 } \alpha + \mu _ { 1 } \gamma{/tex} or {tex}\left( \mu _ { 2 } - \mu _ { 1 } \right) \gamma = \mu _ { 1 } \alpha + \mu _ { 2 } \beta{/tex} ................(i) As {tex}\alpha , \beta{/tex} and {tex}\gamma {/tex} are small and P and N lie close to each other. So, {tex}\alpha \approx \tan \alpha = \frac { A N } { N O } \approx \frac { A N } { P O }{/tex} {tex}\beta \approx \tan \beta = \frac { A N } { N I } \approx \frac { A N } { P I }{/tex} {tex}\gamma \approx \tan \gamma = \frac { A N } { N C } \approx \frac { A N } { P C }{/tex} On using them in equation (i), we obtain {tex}\left( \mu _ { 2 } - \mu _ { 1 } \right) \frac { A N } { P C } = \mu _ { 1 } \frac { A N } { P O } + \mu _ { 2 } \frac { A N } { P I }{/tex} or {tex}\frac { \mu _ { 2 } - \mu _ { 1 } } { P C } = \frac { \mu _ { 1 } } { P O } + \frac { \mu _ { 2 } } { P I }{/tex} ..........(ii) where, PC = +R, radius of curvature PO = -u, object distance PI = +v, image distance So, {tex}\frac { \mu _ { 2 } - \mu _ { 1 } } { R } = \frac { \mu _ { 1 } } { - u } + \frac { \mu _ { 2 } } { v } \text { or } \frac { \mu _ { 2 } - \mu _ { 1 } } { R } = \frac { \mu _ { 2 } } { v } - \frac { \mu _ { 1 } } { u }{/tex} This gives formula for refraction at spherical surface, when object is in rarer medium.
solution_html:
In case of refraction at convex spherical surface, when object is in rarer medium, the image is formed real.

In {tex}\triangle{/tex}OAC, i = a + {tex}\gamma {/tex} and in {tex}\Delta A I C , \gamma = r + \beta \text { or } r = \gamma - \beta{/tex}
Therefore, By snell's law, we have {tex}^ { 1 } \mu _ { 2 } = \frac { \sin i } { \sin r } \approx \frac { i } { r } = \frac { \alpha + \gamma } { \gamma - \beta }{/tex}
or {tex}\frac { \mu _ { 2 } } { \mu _ { 1 } } = \frac { \alpha + \gamma } { \gamma - \beta } \text { or } \mu _ { 2 } \gamma - \mu _ { 2 } \beta = \mu _ { 1 } \alpha + \mu _ { 1 } \gamma{/tex}
or {tex}\left( \mu _ { 2 } - \mu _ { 1 } \right) \gamma = \mu _ { 1 } \alpha + \mu _ { 2 } \beta{/tex} ................(i)
As {tex}\alpha , \beta{/tex} and {tex}\gamma {/tex} are small and P and N lie close to each other.
So, {tex}\alpha \approx \tan \alpha = \frac { A N } { N O } \approx \frac { A N } { P O }{/tex}
{tex}\beta \approx \tan \beta = \frac { A N } { N I } \approx \frac { A N } { P I }{/tex}
{tex}\gamma \approx \tan \gamma = \frac { A N } { N C } \approx \frac { A N } { P C }{/tex}
On using them in equation (i), we obtain
{tex}\left( \mu _ { 2 } - \mu _ { 1 } \right) \frac { A N } { P C } = \mu _ { 1 } \frac { A N } { P O } + \mu _ { 2 } \frac { A N } { P I }{/tex}
or {tex}\frac { \mu _ { 2 } - \mu _ { 1 } } { P C } = \frac { \mu _ { 1 } } { P O } + \frac { \mu _ { 2 } } { P I }{/tex} ..........(ii)
where, PC = +R, radius of curvature
PO = -u, object distance
PI = +v, image distance
So, {tex}\frac { \mu _ { 2 } - \mu _ { 1 } } { R } = \frac { \mu _ { 1 } } { - u } + \frac { \mu _ { 2 } } { v } \text { or } \frac { \mu _ { 2 } - \mu _ { 1 } } { R } = \frac { \mu _ { 2 } } { v } - \frac { \mu _ { 1 } } { u }{/tex}
This gives formula for refraction at spherical surface, when object is in rarer medium.

1. Draw a labelled ray diagram to obtain the real image formed by an astronomical telescope in normal adjustment position. Define its magnifying power.
  2. You are given three lenses of power 0.5 D, 4D and 10 D to design a telescope.
    1. Which lenses should be used as objective and eyepiece? Justify your answer.
    2. Why is the aperture of the objective preferred to be large?
question_text: Draw a labelled ray diagram to obtain the real image formed by an astronomical telescope in normal adjustment position. Define its magnifying power. You are given three lenses of power 0.5 D, 4D and 10 D to design a telescope. Which lenses should be used as objective and eyepiece? Justify your answer. Why is the aperture of the objective preferred to be large?
explanation: In astronomical telescope for normal adjustment, final image is formed at infinity and it is virtual. The labelled ray diagram to obtain one of the real image formed by the astronomical telescope is shown below: Magnifying power is defined as the ratio of the angle subtended at the eye by the focal image as seen through the telescope to the angle subtended at the eye by the object seen directly, when both the image and the object lies at infinity. We know the objective lens of a telescope should have larger focal length and eyepiece lens should have smaller focal length. And focal length is inverse of power, so lens of power (P = 1/f). Thus, 10D can be used as eyepiece and lens of power 0.5 D can be used as objective lens. The objective lens of a telescope should have larger aperture, in order to form bright image of distant objects, so that it can gather sufficient light rays from the distant objects.

question_html:
Read the following paragraph and answer the questions that follow. (4 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 = 4)
When light travels from an optically denser medium to an optically rarer medium, at the interface it is partly reflected back into the same medium and partly refracted to the second medium. The angle of incidence corresponding to an angle of refraction {tex}90^{\circ}{/tex} is called the critical angle ({tex}{i}_{{c}}{/tex}) for the given pair of media. This angle is related to the refractive index of medium 1 with respect to medium 2.
Refraction of light through a prism involves refraction at two plane interfaces. A relation for the refractive index of the material of the prism can be obtained in terms of the refracting angle of the prism and the angle of minimum deviation. For a thin prism, this relation reduces to a simple equation.
Laws of refraction are also valid for refraction of light at a spherical interface. When an object is placed in front of a spherical surface separating two media, its image is formed. A relation between object and image distance, in terms of refractive indices of two media and the radius of curvature of the spherical surface can be obtained. Using this relation for two surfaces of a lens, 'lens maker formula' is obtained.

  1. A small bulb is placed at the bottom of a tank containing a transparent liquid (refractive index n) to a depth H. The radius of the circular area of the surface of liquid, through which light from the bulb can emerge out, is R. Then {tex}\left(\frac{{R}}{{H}}\right){/tex} is: (1)
    1. {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{{n}^2-1}}{/tex}
    2. {tex}\sqrt{{n}^2-1}{/tex}
    3. {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{{n}^2+1}}{/tex}
    4. {tex}\sqrt{{n}^2+1}{/tex}
    1. A parallel beam of light is incident on a face of a prism with refracting angle {tex}60^{\circ}{/tex}. The angle of minimum deviation is found to be {tex}30^{\circ}{/tex}. The refractive index of the material of the prism is close to: (1)
      1. {tex}1.3{/tex}
      2. {tex}1.4{/tex}
      3. 1.5
      4. 1.6

        OR

    2. The angle of minimum deviation for a ray of light incident on a thin prism, made of crown glass {tex}(n=1.52){/tex} is {tex}D_m{/tex}. If the prism was made of dense flint glass ({tex}{n}=1.62{/tex}) instead of crown glass, the angle of minimum deviation will: (1)
      1. decrease by {tex}4 \%{/tex}
      2. increase by {tex}4 \%{/tex}
      3. decrease by 19%
      4. increase by 19%
  3. An object is placed in front of a convex spherical glass surface ({tex}{n}=1.5{/tex} and radius of curvature R) at a distance of 4 R from it. As the object is moved slowly close to the surface, the image formed is: (1)
    1. always real
    2. always virtual
    3. first real and then virtual

question_text: Read the following paragraph and answer the questions that follow. (4 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 = 4) When light travels from an optically denser medium to an optically rarer medium, at the interface it is partly reflected back into the same medium and partly refracted to the second medium. The angle of incidence corresponding to an angle of refraction {tex}90^{\circ}{/tex} is called the critical angle ( {tex}{i}_{{c}}{/tex} ) for the given pair of media. This angle is related to the refractive index of medium 1 with respect to medium 2. Refraction of light through a prism involves refraction at two plane interfaces. A relation for the refractive index of the material of the prism can be obtained in terms of the refracting angle of the prism and the angle of minimum deviation. For a thin prism, this relation reduces to a simple equation. Laws of refraction are also valid for refraction of light at a spherical interface. When an object is placed in front of a spherical surface separating two media, its image is formed. A relation between object and image distance, in terms of refractive indices of two media and the radius of curvature of the spherical surface can be obtained. Using this relation for two surfaces of a lens, 'lens maker formula' is obtained. A small bulb is placed at the bottom of a tank containing a transparent liquid (refractive index n) to a depth H. The radius of the circular area of the surface of liquid, through which light from the bulb can emerge out, is R. Then {tex}\left(\frac{{R}}{{H}}\right){/tex} is: (1) {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{{n}^2-1}}{/tex} {tex}\sqrt{{n}^2-1}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{{n}^2+1}}{/tex} {tex}\sqrt{{n}^2+1}{/tex} A parallel beam of light is incident on a face of a prism with refracting angle {tex}60^{\circ}{/tex} . The angle of minimum deviation is found to be {tex}30^{\circ}{/tex} . question_text: Draw a ray diagram to show how the final image is formed at infinity in an astronomical refracting telescope. Obtain an expression for its magnifying power. Two thin lenses L 1 and L 2 , L 1 being a convex lens of focal length 24 cm and L 2 a concave lens of focal length 18 cm are placed coaxially at a separation of 45 cm. A 1 cm tall object is placed in front of the lens L 1 at a distance of 36 cm. Find the location and height of the image formed by the combination.
explanation: From the diagram {tex}\beta=\frac{h}{f_e}{/tex} and {tex}\alpha=\frac{h}{f_o}{/tex} Magnifying Power = {tex}\frac{f_O}{f_e}{/tex} Fr lens L 1 , {tex}\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{-36}=\frac{1}{24}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{v}=\frac{1}{24}-\frac{1}{36}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{v}=\frac{3-2}{72}=\frac{1}{72}{/tex} v = 72 cm For lens L 2 : {tex}\frac{1}{v^{\prime}}-\frac{1}{u^{\prime}}=\frac{1}{f^{\prime}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{v^{\prime}}-\frac{1}{(72-45)}=\frac{1}{-18}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{v^{\prime}}=\frac{1}{-18}+\frac{1}{27}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{v^{\prime}}=\frac{-3+2}{54}=\frac{-1}{54}{/tex} v = -54 cm Final distance {tex}v_1^{\prime}{/tex} = -54 - (-45) {tex}v_1^{\prime}{/tex} = -9 cm (to the left of convex lens) Magnification {tex}\frac{h_i}{h_o}=\frac{v_1^{\prime}}{u}{/tex} {tex}\frac{h_i}{1}=\frac{-9}{-36} \Rightarrow{/tex} h i = + {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex} cm hence,height of the image formed {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex} cm
solution: From the diagram {tex}\beta=\frac{h}{f_e}{/tex} and {tex}\alpha=\frac{h}{f_o}{/tex} Magnifying Power = {tex}\frac{f_O}{f_e}{/tex} Fr lens L 1 , {tex}\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{-36}=\frac{1}{24}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{v}=\frac{1}{24}-\frac{1}{36}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{v}=\frac{3-2}{72}=\frac{1}{72}{/tex} v = 72 cm For lens L 2 : {tex}\frac{1}{v^{\prime}}-\frac{1}{u^{\prime}}=\frac{1}{f^{\prime}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{v^{\prime}}-\frac{1}{(72-45)}=\frac{1}{-18}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{v^{\prime}}=\frac{1}{-18}+\frac{1}{27}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{v^{\prime}}=\frac{-3+2}{54}=\frac{-1}{54}{/tex} v = -54 cm Final distance {tex}v_1^{\prime}{/tex} = -54 - (-45) {tex}v_1^{\prime}{/tex} = -9 cm (to the left of convex lens) Magnification {tex}\frac{h_i}{h_o}=\frac{v_1^{\prime}}{u}{/tex} {tex}\frac{h_i}{1}=\frac{-9}{-36} \Rightarrow{/tex} h i = + {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex} cm hence,height of the image formed {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex} cm
solution_html:
1. From the diagram {tex}\beta=\frac{h}{f_e}{/tex} and {tex}\alpha=\frac{h}{f_o}{/tex}
    Magnifying Power = {tex}\frac{f_O}{f_e}{/tex}

  2. Fr lens L1,
    {tex}\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f}{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{-36}=\frac{1}{24}{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{1}{v}=\frac{1}{24}-\frac{1}{36}{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{1}{v}=\frac{3-2}{72}=\frac{1}{72}{/tex}
    v = 72 cm
    For lens L2:
    {tex}\frac{1}{v^{\prime}}-\frac{1}{u^{\prime}}=\frac{1}{f^{\prime}}{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{1}{v^{\prime}}-\frac{1}{(72-45)}=\frac{1}{-18}{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{1}{v^{\prime}}=\frac{1}{-18}+\frac{1}{27}{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{1}{v^{\prime}}=\frac{-3+2}{54}=\frac{-1}{54}{/tex}
    v = -54 cm

    {tex}v_1^{\prime}{/tex} = -9 cm (to the left of convex lens)

1. Prove that for a concave mirror, the radius of curvature is twice the focal length for small aperture as compare to its radius of curvature.
  2. With the help of a suitable diagram, derive the mirror formula for a concave mirror.
question_text: Prove that for a concave mirror, the radius of curvature is twice the focal length for small aperture as compare to its radius of curvature. With the help of a suitable diagram, derive the mirror formula for a concave mirror.
explanation: Let us consider MM' as small aperture concave mirror. The line through centre of curvature MC is perpendicular to the mirror. Applying law of reflection, {tex}\angle O M C=\angle C M F{/tex} ( angle of incidence = angle of reflection) {tex}\angle M C F=\angle O M C{/tex} (alternate angles) Hence {tex}\angle M C F=\angle C M F{/tex} So, CF = MF Since aperture of mirror is very small, D is very near to P and we can put CF = FP PC = 2PF R = 2f Or, {tex}f=\frac{R}{2}{/tex} Triangle A 'B' F and MPF are similar Therefore, {tex}\frac{B^{\prime} A^{\prime}}{P M}=\frac{B^{\prime} F}{F P}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{B^{\prime} A^{\prime}}{B A}=\frac{B^{\prime} F}{F P}{/tex} ( {tex}\because{/tex} PM = AB) ...(1) Since < APB = < A'PB, the right-angled triangle A'B'P and ABP are also similar, Therefore, {tex}\frac{B^{\prime} A^{\prime}}{B A}=\frac{B^{\prime} P}{B P}{/tex} ...(2) From equation (1) and (2) {tex}\frac{B^{\prime} F}{F P}=\frac{B^{\prime} P-F P}{F P}=\frac{B^{\prime} P}{B P}{/tex} ...(3) Apply the sign convention That is B'P = -V FP = -f BP = -u using the relation equation (3) become {tex}\frac{-v+f}{-f}=\frac{-v}{-u}{/tex} {tex}\frac{v-f}{f}=\frac{v}{u}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{v}+\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f}{/tex} This relation is known as mirror equation where, v = image distance u = object distances f = focal length
solution: Let us consider MM' as small aperture concave mirror. The line through centre of curvature MC is perpendicular to the mirror. Applying law of reflection, {tex}\angle O M C=\angle C M F{/tex} ( angle of incidence = angle of reflection) {tex}\angle M C F=\angle O M C{/tex} (alternate angles) Hence {tex}\angle M C F=\angle C M F{/tex} So, CF = MF Since aperture of mirror is very small, D is very near to P and we can put CF = FP PC = 2PF R = 2f Or, {tex}f=\frac{R}{2}{/tex} Triangle A 'B' F and MPF are similar Therefore, {tex}\frac{B^{\prime} A^{\prime}}{P M}=\frac{B^{\prime} F}{F P}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{B^{\prime} A^{\prime}}{B A}=\frac{B^{\prime} F}{F P}{/tex} ( {tex}\because{/tex} PM = AB) ...(1) Since < APB = < A'PB, the right-angled triangle A'B'P and ABP are also similar, Therefore, {tex}\frac{B^{\prime} A^{\prime}}{B A}=\frac{B^{\prime} P}{B P}{/tex} ...(2) From equation (1) and (2) {tex}\frac{B^{\prime} F}{F P}=\frac{B^{\prime} P-F P}{F P}=\frac{B^{\prime} P}{B P}{/tex} ...(3) Apply the sign convention That is B'P = -V FP = -f BP = -u using the relation equation (3) become {tex}\frac{-v+f}{-f}=\frac{-v}{-u}{/tex} {tex}\frac{v-f}{f}=\frac{v}{u}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{v}+\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f}{/tex} This relation is known as mirror equation where, v = image distance u = object distances f = focal length
solution_html:
1. Let us consider MM' as small aperture concave mirror. The line through centre of curvature MC is perpendicular to the mirror. Applying law of reflection,
    {tex}\angle O M C=\angle C M F{/tex}
    ( angle of incidence = angle of reflection)
    {tex}\angle M C F=\angle O M C{/tex} (alternate angles)
    Hence {tex}\angle M C F=\angle C M F{/tex}
    So, CF = MF
    Since aperture of mirror is very small, D is very near to P and we can put
    CF = FP
    PC = 2PF
    R = 2f
    Or, {tex}f=\frac{R}{2}{/tex}


  2. Triangle A 'B' F and MPF are similar
    Therefore,
    {tex}\frac{B^{\prime} A^{\prime}}{P M}=\frac{B^{\prime} F}{F P}{/tex}
    or {tex}\frac{B^{\prime} A^{\prime}}{B A}=\frac{B^{\prime} F}{F P}{/tex} ({tex}\because{/tex} PM = AB) ...(1)
    Since < APB = < A'PB, the right-angled triangle A'B'P and ABP are also similar, Therefore,
    {tex}\frac{B^{\prime} A^{\prime}}{B A}=\frac{B^{\prime} P}{B P}{/tex} ...(2)
    From equation (1) and (2)
    {tex}\frac{B^{\prime} F}{F P}=\frac{B^{\prime} P-F P}{F P}=\frac{B^{\prime} P}{B P}{/tex} ...(3)
    Apply the sign convention
    That is B'P = -V
    FP = -f
    BP = -u
    using the relation equation (3) become
    {tex}\frac{-v+f}{-f}=\frac{-v}{-u}{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{v-f}{f}=\frac{v}{u}{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{1}{v}+\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f}{/tex}
    This relation is known as mirror equation
    where, v = image distance
    u = object distances
    f = focal length

question_text: Two converging lenses of focal lengths f 1 and f 2 are placed coaxially in contact. Derive the expression for the focal length of the combination using the ray diagram for the formation of the image of an object by the combination. The figure shows the variation of image distance v with object distance u for a lens. What is the nature of the lens? Using the graph, find the focal length of the lens.
explanation: Image formed by the first lens A, {tex}\frac{1}{v_1}-\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f_1}{/tex} ...(i) Image formed by second lens B, {tex}\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{v_1}=\frac{1}{f_2}{/tex} ...(ii) Adding eq (i) and (ii), we get, {tex}\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f_1}+\frac{1}{f_2}{/tex} ...(iii) If the two lens system is regarded as equivalent to a single lens of focal length f, we have {tex}\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f}{/tex} ...(iv) From equation (iii) and (iv) ,we get {tex}\frac{1}{f}=\frac{1}{f_1}+\frac{1}{f_2}{/tex} From graph we see that as the object moves away from the lens the image comes nearer. which means it is a convex lens. u = -20 cm v = {tex}\frac{40}{3}{/tex} cm {tex}\frac{1}{f}=\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{u}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{3}{40}+\frac{1}{20}{/tex} f = 8 cm Hence, the focal length of the lens 8 cm.
solution: Image formed by the first lens A, {tex}\frac{1}{v_1}-\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f_1}{/tex} ...(i) Image formed by second lens B, {tex}\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{v_1}=\frac{1}{f_2}{/tex} ...(ii) Adding eq (i) and (ii), we get, {tex}\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f_1}+\frac{1}{f_2}{/tex} ...(iii) If the two lens system is regarded as equivalent to a single lens of focal length f, we have {tex}\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f}{/tex} ...(iv) From equation (iii) and (iv) ,we get {tex}\frac{1}{f}=\frac{1}{f_1}+\frac{1}{f_2}{/tex} From graph we see that as the object moves away from the lens the image comes nearer. which means it is a convex lens. u = -20 cm v = {tex}\frac{40}{3}{/tex} cm {tex}\frac{1}{f}=\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{u}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{3}{40}+\frac{1}{20}{/tex} f = 8 cm Hence, the focal length of the lens 8 cm.
solution_html:
1. Image formed by the first lens A,
    {tex}\frac{1}{v_1}-\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f_1}{/tex} ...(i)
    Image formed by second lens B,
    {tex}\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{v_1}=\frac{1}{f_2}{/tex} ...(ii)
    Adding eq (i) and (ii), we get,
    {tex}\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f_1}+\frac{1}{f_2}{/tex} ...(iii)
    If the two lens system is regarded as equivalent to a single lens of focal length f, we have
    {tex}\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f}{/tex} ...(iv)
    From equation (iii) and (iv) ,we get
    {tex}\frac{1}{f}=\frac{1}{f_1}+\frac{1}{f_2}{/tex}
    1. From graph we see that as the object moves away from the lens the image comes nearer. which means it is a convex lens.
    2. u = -20 cm
      v = {tex}\frac{40}{3}{/tex} cm
      {tex}\frac{1}{f}=\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{u}{/tex}
      {tex}=\frac{3}{40}+\frac{1}{20}{/tex}
      f = 8 cm
      Hence, the focal length of the lens 8 cm.

question_html:
With the help of ray diagram, show the formation of image of a point object by refraction of light at a spherical surface separating two media of refractive indices n1 and n2 (n2 > n1) respectively. Using this diagram, derive the relation {tex}\frac{{{n_2}}}{v} - \frac{{{n_1}}}{u} = \frac{{{n_2} - {n_1}}}{R}{/tex}. Write the sign conventions used.
What happens to the focal length of convex lens when it is immersed in water?
question_text: With the help of ray diagram, show the formation of image of a point object by refraction of light at a spherical surface separating two media of refractive indices n 1 and n 2 (n 2 > n 1 ) respectively. Using this diagram, derive the relation {tex}\frac{{{n_2}}}{v} - \frac{{{n_1}}}{u} = \frac{{{n_2} - {n_1}}}{R}{/tex} . Write the sign conventions used. What happens to the focal length of convex lens when it is immersed in water?
explanation: AMB is a convex surface separating two media of refractive indices n 1 and n 2 (n 2 > n 1 ). Consider a point object O placed on the principal axis. A ray ON is incident at N and refracts along NI. The ray along ON goes straight and meets the previous ray at I. Thus I is the real image of O. From Snell's law, {tex}\frac {n_2}{n_1} = \frac{{\sin i}}{{\sin r}}{/tex} n 1 sin i = n 2 sin r or n 1 i = n 2 r [ {tex}\because \sin \theta \cong \theta {\text{ as }}\theta{/tex} is very small] From {tex}\Delta NOC,i = \alpha + \gamma{/tex} From {tex}\Delta NIC,\gamma = r - \beta{/tex} or {tex}r = \gamma - \beta{/tex} {tex}\therefore {n_1}(\alpha + \gamma ) = {n_2}(\gamma - \beta ){/tex} or {tex}{n_1}\alpha + {n_2}\beta = ({n_2} - {n_1})\gamma{/tex} But {tex}\alpha \cong \tan \alpha = \frac{{NP}}{{OP}} = \frac{{NP}}{{OM}}{/tex} [P is close to M] {tex}\beta \cong \tan \beta = \frac{{NP}}{{PI}} = \frac{{NP}}{{MI}}{/tex} {tex}\gamma \cong \tan \gamma = \frac{{NP}}{{PC}} = \frac{{NP}}{{MC}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore {n_1} \cdot \frac{{NP}}{{OM}} + {n_2} \cdot \frac{{NP}}{{MI}} = ({n_2} - {n_1})\frac{{NP}}{{MC}}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{{{n_1}}}{{OM}} + \frac{{{n_2}}}{{MI}} = \frac{{{n_2} - {n_1}}}{{MC}}{/tex} Using Cartesian sign convention, OM = -u, MI = +v, MC = +R {tex}\therefore \frac{{{n_1}}}{{ - u}} + \frac{{{n_2}}}{v} = \frac{{{n_2} - {n_1}}}{R}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{{{n_2}}}{v} - \frac{{{n_1}}}{u} = \frac{{{n_2} - {n_1}}}{R}{/tex} The lens maker formula gives us the relationship, {tex}\frac1f=(\mu-1)(\frac1{R_1}-\frac1{R_2}){/tex} i.e. focal length, f and refractive index, {tex}\mu{/tex} have inverse dependence. Now, as refractive index of water is greater than the air, the focal length of the lens will reduce when immersed in water.
solution: AMB is a convex surface separating two media of refractive indices n 1 and n 2 (n 2 > n 1 ). Consider a point object O placed on the principal axis. A ray ON is incident at N and refracts along NI. The ray along ON goes straight and meets the previous ray at I. Thus I is the real image of O. From Snell's law, {tex}\frac {n_2}{n_1} = \frac{{\sin i}}{{\sin r}}{/tex} n 1 sin i = n 2 sin r or n 1 i = n 2 r [ {tex}\because \sin \theta \cong \theta {\text{ as }}\theta{/tex} is very small] From {tex}\Delta NOC,i = \alpha + \gamma{/tex} From {tex}\Delta NIC,\gamma = r - \beta{/tex} or {tex}r = \gamma - \beta{/tex} {tex}\therefore {n_1}(\alpha + \gamma ) = {n_2}(\gamma - \beta ){/tex} or {tex}{n_1}\alpha + {n_2}\beta = ({n_2} - {n_1})\gamma{/tex} But {tex}\alpha \cong \tan \alpha = \frac{{NP}}{{OP}} = \frac{{NP}}{{OM}}{/tex} [P is close to M] {tex}\beta \cong \tan \beta = \frac{{NP}}{{PI}} = \frac{{NP}}{{MI}}{/tex} {tex}\gamma \cong \tan \gamma = \frac{{NP}}{{PC}} = \frac{{NP}}{{MC}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore {n_1} \cdot \frac{{NP}}{{OM}} + {n_2} \cdot \frac{{NP}}{{MI}} = ({n_2} - {n_1})\frac{{NP}}{{MC}}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{{{n_1}}}{{OM}} + \frac{{{n_2}}}{{MI}} = \frac{{{n_2} - {n_1}}}{{MC}}{/tex} Using Cartesian sign convention, OM = -u, MI = +v, MC = +R {tex}\therefore \frac{{{n_1}}}{{ - u}} + \frac{{{n_2}}}{v} = \frac{{{n_2} - {n_1}}}{R}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{{{n_2}}}{v} - \frac{{{n_1}}}{u} = \frac{{{n_2} - {n_1}}}{R}{/tex} The lens maker formula gives us the relationship, {tex}\frac1f=(\mu-1)(\frac1{R_1}-\frac1{R_2}){/tex} i.e. focal length, f and refractive index, {tex}\mu{/tex} have inverse dependence. Now, as refractive index of water is greater than the air, the focal length of the lens will reduce when immersed in water.
solution_html:
AMB is a convex surface separating two media of refractive indices n1 and n2 (n2 > n1). Consider a point object O placed on the principal axis. A ray ON is incident at N and refracts along NI. The ray along ON goes straight and meets the previous ray at I. Thus I is the real image of O.

From Snell's law,
{tex}\frac {n_2}{n_1} = \frac{{\sin i}}{{\sin r}}{/tex}
n1sin i = n2sin r
or n1i = n2r [{tex}\because \sin \theta \cong \theta {\text{ as }}\theta{/tex} is very small]
From {tex}\Delta NOC,i = \alpha + \gamma{/tex}
From {tex}\Delta NIC,\gamma = r - \beta{/tex}
or {tex}r = \gamma - \beta{/tex}
{tex}\therefore {n_1}(\alpha + \gamma ) = {n_2}(\gamma - \beta ){/tex}
or {tex}{n_1}\alpha + {n_2}\beta = ({n_2} - {n_1})\gamma{/tex}
But {tex}\alpha \cong \tan \alpha = \frac{{NP}}{{OP}} = \frac{{NP}}{{OM}}{/tex} [P is close to M]
{tex}\beta \cong \tan \beta = \frac{{NP}}{{PI}} = \frac{{NP}}{{MI}}{/tex}
{tex}\gamma \cong \tan \gamma = \frac{{NP}}{{PC}} = \frac{{NP}}{{MC}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore {n_1} \cdot \frac{{NP}}{{OM}} + {n_2} \cdot \frac{{NP}}{{MI}} = ({n_2} - {n_1})\frac{{NP}}{{MC}}{/tex}
or {tex}\frac{{{n_1}}}{{OM}} + \frac{{{n_2}}}{{MI}} = \frac{{{n_2} - {n_1}}}{{MC}}{/tex}
Using Cartesian sign convention,
OM = -u, MI = +v, MC = +R
{tex}\therefore \frac{{{n_1}}}{{ - u}} + \frac{{{n_2}}}{v} = \frac{{{n_2} - {n_1}}}{R}{/tex}
or {tex}\frac{{{n_2}}}{v} - \frac{{{n_1}}}{u} = \frac{{{n_2} - {n_1}}}{R}{/tex}
The lens maker formula gives us the relationship,
{tex}\frac1f=(\mu-1)(\frac1{R_1}-\frac1{R_2}){/tex}
i.e. focal length, f and refractive index, {tex}\mu{/tex} have inverse dependence.
Now, as refractive index of water is greater than the air, the focal length of the lens will reduce when immersed in water.

1. Draw the labelled ray diagram for the formation of image by an astronomical telescope.
  2. Derive the expression for its magnifying power in normal adjustment. Write two basic features which can distinguish between a telescope and a compound microscope.
question_text: Draw the labelled ray diagram for the formation of image by an astronomical telescope. Derive the expression for its magnifying power in normal adjustment. Write two basic features which can distinguish between a telescope and a compound microscope.
explanation: Astronomical Telescope: Magnifying power of astronomical telescope in normal adjustment is defined as the ratio of the angle subtended at the eye by the final image to the angle subtended at the eye, by the object directly, when the final image and the object both lie at infinite distance from the eye. Magnifying power, {tex}m = \frac{\beta }{\alpha }{/tex} ..........(1) As angles α and β are small, therefore, α ≈ tan α and β ≈ tan β From equation (1), {tex}m = \frac{tan \beta }{tan \alpha }{/tex} ..........(2) In {tex}\Delta A'B'E,{/tex} {tex}\tan \beta = \frac{{A'B'}}{{{E}B'}}{/tex} In {tex}\Delta A'B'O,{/tex} {tex}\tan \alpha = \frac{{A'B'}}{{{O}B'}}{/tex} Put in equation (2), we get, {tex}m = \frac{{A'B'}}{{{E}B'}} \times \frac{{{O}B'}}{{A'B'}} = \frac{{{O}B'}}{{{E}B'}}{/tex} or {tex}m = \frac{{{f_0}}}{{ - {f_e}}}{/tex} Where OB' = f 0 focal length of objective lens, EB' = -f e focal length of eye lens. Negative sign of m indicates that final image is inverted. The diameter of objective is kept large to increase (i) intensity of image, (ii) resolving power of telescope. Telescope Compound Microscope Objective lens is of large focal length and eye lens is small focal length. Both objective and eye lenses are of small focal length but focal length of eye lens is larger than that of objective lens. Objective is of very large aperature. Objective is of small aperture.

1. When a ray of light passes through a triangular glass prism, find out the relation for the total deviation, {tex} \delta{/tex} in terms of the angle of incidence, i and angle of emergence, e.
  2. Plot a graph showing the variation of angle of deviation with the angle of incidence and obtain the condition for the angle of minimum deviation.
question_text: When a ray of light passes through a triangular glass prism, find out the relation for the total deviation, {tex} \delta{/tex} in terms of the angle of incidence, i and angle of emergence, e. Plot a graph showing the variation of angle of deviation with the angle of incidence and obtain the condition for the angle of minimum deviation.
explanation: Let PQ and RS are incident and emergent rays. Let incident ray get deviated by {tex}\delta{/tex} by the prism. i.e. {tex}\angle T M S = \delta{/tex} Let {tex}\delta _ { 1 }{/tex} and {tex}\delta _ { 2}{/tex} are deviation produced at refractions taking place at AB and AC, respectively. {tex}\therefore \quad \delta = \delta _ { 1 } + \delta _ { 2 } = \left( i - r _ { 1 } \right) + \left( e - r _ { 2 } \right){/tex} = (i + e) - (r 1 + r 2 ) .......(i) But in {tex}\triangle{/tex} QNR, {tex}\angle Q N R + \angle R Q N + \angle Q R N = 180 ^ { \circ }{/tex} or {tex}\angle Q N R = 180 ^ { \circ } - \left( r _ { 1 } + r _ { 2 } \right){/tex} .......(ii) In {tex}\square {\text{QARN, }}\angle {\text{AQN and }}\angle {\text{ARN}}{/tex} are right angles. So, {tex}\angle Q N R = 180 ^ { \circ } - A{/tex} ......(iii) where, A is angle of prism. From Eqs. (ii) and (iii), we have A = r 1 + r 2 .......(iv) From Eqs. (i) and (iv), we have {tex}\delta = ( i + e ) - A{/tex} {tex}i - \delta{/tex} graph is shown in the figure below: The conditions for the angle of minimum deviation are given as below: i. Angle of incidence (i) and angle of emergence (e) are equal, i.e. {tex}\angle i = \angle e{/tex} ii. In equilateral prism, the refracted ray is parallel to base of prism. iii. The incident and emergent rays are bent on same angle from refracting surfaces of the prism, i.e. {tex}\angle r _ { 1 } = \angle r _ { 2 }{/tex} For minimum deviation position, Putting r = r 1 = r 2 in Eq. (iv), we get, {tex}2 r = A \Rightarrow r = \frac { A } { 2 }{/tex} Also, for minimum deviation, i = e, Thus, {tex}\delta _ { m } = 2 i - A{/tex} {tex}i = \frac { A + \delta _ { m } } { 2 }{/tex} {tex}\therefore {/tex} Refractive index of material of prism is {tex}\mu = \frac { \sin i } { \sin r }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \mu = \frac { \sin \left( \frac { A + \delta _ { m } } { 2 } \right) } { \sin \frac{A}{ 2 }}{/tex}

solution_html:
1. Let PQ and RS are incident and emergent rays. Let incident ray get deviated by {tex}\delta{/tex} by the prism. i.e. {tex}\angle T M S = \delta{/tex}
    Let {tex}\delta _ { 1 }{/tex} and {tex}\delta _ { 2}{/tex} are deviation produced at refractions taking place at AB and AC, respectively.

    {tex}\therefore \quad \delta = \delta _ { 1 } + \delta _ { 2 } = \left( i - r _ { 1 } \right) + \left( e - r _ { 2 } \right){/tex}
    = (i + e) - (r1 + r2) .......(i)
    But in {tex}\triangle{/tex}QNR,
    {tex}\angle Q N R + \angle R Q N + \angle Q R N = 180 ^ { \circ }{/tex}
    or {tex}\angle Q N R = 180 ^ { \circ } - \left( r _ { 1 } + r _ { 2 } \right){/tex} .......(ii)
    In {tex}\square {\text{QARN, }}\angle {\text{AQN and }}\angle {\text{ARN}}{/tex} are right angles.
    So, {tex}\angle Q N R = 180 ^ { \circ } - A{/tex} ......(iii)
    where, A is angle of prism.
    From Eqs. (ii) and (iii), we have
    A = r1 + r2 .......(iv)
    From Eqs. (i) and (iv), we have
    {tex}\delta = ( i + e ) - A{/tex}
     
  2. {tex}i - \delta{/tex} graph is shown in the figure below:

    The conditions for the angle of minimum deviation are given as below:
    i. Angle of incidence (i) and angle of emergence (e) are equal, i.e. {tex}\angle i = \angle e{/tex}
    ii. In equilateral prism, the refracted ray is parallel to base of prism.
    iii. The incident and emergent rays are bent on same angle from refracting surfaces of the prism, i.e. {tex}\angle r _ { 1 } = \angle r _ { 2 }{/tex}
    For minimum deviation position,
    Putting r = r1 = r2 in Eq. (iv), we get,
    {tex}2 r = A \Rightarrow r = \frac { A } { 2 }{/tex}
    Also, for minimum deviation, i = e,
    Thus, {tex}\delta _ { m } = 2 i - A{/tex}
    {tex}i = \frac { A + \delta _ { m } } { 2 }{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore {/tex} Refractive index of material of prism is 
    {tex}\mu = \frac { \sin i } { \sin r }{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \mu = \frac { \sin \left( \frac { A + \delta _ { m } } { 2 } \right) } { \sin \frac{A}{ 2 }}{/tex}
